name: Project Leo
info: AVAIO Digital Group has announced a $6 billion data center hub outside Little Rock, supposedly off 145th Street. AVAIO is currently contracted with Entergy Arkansas for 150 MW of power, but they anticipate power demand of up to 1 GW as the facility grows. According to the announcement, construction of the first phase of the project is expected to start in the first quarter of 2026 and will be complete and energized in June 2027.

name: Project Pyramid
info: Google is proposing a five building hyperscale data center campus on a 1,178-acre property representing a $4-10-billion investment. The data center campus will reportedly only take up around ~580 acres of the properties. The project received approval for a 30-year payment-in-lieu-of-taxes (PILOT) agreement. Simultaneously, Google plans to construct a 600 MW solar farm to power this proposal, and the Arkansas Public Service Commission’s approved a 350 MW energy storage system.

name: Clarksville Data Center
address: South of the Johnson Regional Medical Center
info: Serverfarm is building a $8 billion hyperscale data center on 135 acres near the Johnson Regional Medical Center. This campus would be composed of six 360,000-square-foot buildings, each at ~64 MW.

name: Port of Little Rock Data Center
address: Located in unnamed tributaries and adjacent wetlands to Fourche Bayou
info: Google and Willowbend Capital, LLC are proposing a data center campus comprised of five 285,300-square-foot buildings, totaling ~1.43 million square feet in total. The project is expected to draw more than 100 MW of power. The proposal also includes two office/support buildings and an electrical substation/switching station covering around 20 acres.

name: Vilonia Cryptomine
companies: Interstate Holdings Blockchain; Steve Landers Jr.
info: A proposal from Interstate Holdings Blockchain for a cryptomine was rejected and withdrawn near the Entergy substation in Vilonia.

name: Millstone Nuclear Power Station Data Center
address: On site of Millstone Nuclear Power Station
companies: NE Edge Data Centers; Dominion Energy
info: NE Edge Data Centers is proposing a data center complex on site of the Millstone Nuclear Power Station, allowing the developer to purchase and draw electricity directly from the plant, which would occupy less than 5% of Millstone’s 526-acre site. The proposal consists of an electric switchyard and two 2-story data center facilities spanning an area of roughly 1.5 million square feet.

name: Wallingford Data Center
address: Watershed Interchange District at 1181 Barnes Road
info: Charter Development, as Charter Development Group LLC, submitted an application to modify Wallingford's zoning for watershed interchange (WI) district zones to include data centers as a permitted use in 2024.

name: Bloomfield Data Center Proposal
companies: Atlas Capital Group / Gotspace Data LLC
info: Atlas Capital Group is proposing a data center in Bloomfield that would feature a The project would include over 900,000 square feet of data processing space and a 127,000-square-foot substation. This project is anticipated to create ~40 jobs.

name: Trumbull Data Center Investment
info: Not a new construction project, but a large reinvestment ($200M) into an existing data center facility to attract more capacity and AWS/AI tenants; supported by municipality via tax incentive program.

name: Project Washington
info: In March 2026, a state board unanimously upheld a decision from the Environmental Secretary that this project violates the Coastal Zone Act as it is located too close to shorelines. It is delayed while the developer decides to escalate to higher courts or withdraw it. Starwood Digital Ventures has proposed a 1.2 GW, 6-million-square-foot hyperscale data center campus off River Road. This site, which requires zoning change prior to approval, would be comprised of 11 2-story buildings. As of February 2026, the project has been delayed after the Delaware Department of Natural Resources and Environmental Control (DNREC) deemed it not permitted under the Coastal Zone Act due to excessive diesal generator risks. The developer has 14 days to appeal this decision.

name: St. Georges Business Park Data Center Development  / "Frightland Project"
address: Near Port Penn Road; Frightland (Halloween-themed attraction)
info: Parkway Gravel Inc. is proposing the St. Georges Business Park in Middletown, just south of Port Penn Road. The complete project would redevelop nearly ~1500 acres and may require demolition of the Frightland Halloween attraction. Current exploratory plans call for approximately 3.24 million square feet of warehouse space across three large buildings. The southern portion of the property is planned as the Lighthouse Farms residential development, consisting of a mix of townhomes and single-family homes. The commercial buildings are planned as large warehouse facilities; data center use is not specified in the planning documents. The mapped area referenced represents only the northern portion of the overall parcel, where the warehouse buildings are proposed.

name: White Clay Creek Business Park Data Center Redevelopment
address: 100 White Clay Creek Business Park, off Ogletown Road
info: Developers are proposing a redevelopment that would demolish seven existing buildings on a 44-acre site called White Clay Center Industrial Park to replace them with three light industrial buildings and a guard shack for use as a data center campus. Because the property lies outside Newark’s municipal boundaries, it will be reviewed through New Castle County’s development process.

name: Project Zodiac
info: Google was granted a 10 year, 50% tax abatement from the Fort Wayne City Council following rezoning approval for 650 acres of land in Allen County by the Allen County Board of Commissioners in December 2023. The hyperscale data center project called Project Zodiac encompasses a total of 850 acres of land in total. A recent permit request was made acoording to local news to add 140 diesel generators on the site as backup, although there is no public record of an offical capacity for the site.

name: Bristol Data Center
address: South of Rail Park Drive, On County Rd 15 and 23
info: Preliminary activity south of Bristol Indiana has been underway since September 2024 when initial attempts to rezone Wilhelm Farm to M-1 was not passed. However since August 7th, 2025 when the Bristol Town Hall approved rezoning of roughly 170 acres from A-1 to M-1. On March 5th, 2026, the town hall also held a meeting where they discussed annexing 500 acres of agricultural land into Bristol. Residents fear that this land, which lies adjacent to the Wilhelm Farm, will likewise be used for future data centers. No developer has been named at this time.

name: Granger Data Center
address: Off Capital Avenue and Cleveland Rd
info: Microsoft purchased more than 900 acres of land on January 1st, 2025, months before St. Joseph City Hall approved the rezoning of a small section of the land for industrial development. In the same meeting, it also approved for the land to be developed specifically for data centers. Estimates for energy usage currently top 1 GW. Microsoft purchased the land for about $1.8 million.

name: South Bend Data Center
address: Prarie Ave and US-20, South West Corner of South Bend
info: A member of the South Bend Common Committee, Oliver Davis, was presented documents of plans to build a data center on a 300 acre plot in the south west corner of the city of South Bend. The plot of land, owned by Ceres Farms LLC., is already zoned industrial. Local news reports that the developers responsible for the rejected proposal in New Carlisle are the same involved in the South Bend plans, Western Hospitality Partners.

name: Decennial Group Akron Data Center
info: Decennial Group, a real estate investment firm, is proposing a 500 MW data center on 300 acres of land in Akron. The currently no site has been selected or announced publicly.

name: Project Rainier
info: AWS is partnering with Anthropic to supply upwards of half a million Trainium2 chips for training their AI models. The public announcement came in December 2024 during AWS' cloud computing expo, re:Invent. Prior to the official announcement, the St. Joseph County Redevelopment Commission began discussing preliminary activity in a November 9th, 2023. In their original meeting, they passed a resolution permitting Razor5 LLC to perform land development on the lower section of the site, a 680 acre plot. In May, 2024 Razor5 LLC purchased both site locations, totalling 850 acres. Amazon later publicized its deal with NIPSCO, the northern Indiana utility company, to provide their data center campus with 2.4 GW of electricity. Amazon cites that it will ultimately invest upwards of $15 billion into the site once it is fully completed.

name: PowerHouse Louisville
info: PowerHouse Data Centers and Poe Companies are constructing a ~150-acre hyperscale data center campus on Campground Road.

name: Project Lincoln
info: Western Hospitality Partners originally proposed a $6 billion hyperscale data center campus on 267 acres of agriculturally zoned land outside La Grange. However, as of june 2025, the developer withdrew the proposal and instead submitted a new application for a smaller industrial-zoned site ~3.5 miles away. The new proposal site is located next to the Bluegrass Generating Station.

name: Franklin–Simpson Data Storage and Service Center
address: 421 Steele Road, near Exit 2 of Interstate 65
info: TenKey LandCo is proposing a data center on 200 acres off Steele Road, near Interstate 65. Preliminary plans include three 200,000-square-foot buildings that would be built over three years. In late January 2026, the company has filed a lawsuit against Simpson County Planning and Zoning Commission, claiming they lack the legal authority to use state law to impose operational limits for developments located within city limits.

name: Harrodsburg Data Center
info: JLL is advertising a 500-acre plot of private land off Handy Road near Burgin for a hyperscale data center campus. This is a strategic placement given the site's proximity to the E.W. Brown Generating Station and high-voltage transmission lines. This project is in the early stages and there have been no formal application or rezoning requests submitted yet as of mid February 2026.

name: Bates Mill No. 3. Data Center
info: Lewiston City officials denied a proposal for a $300-million data center project on the site of Bates Mill No. 3. This project would have allowed for the development of a 85,000-square-foot data center. If the developer appeals this decision, the project will be relisted as delayed.

name: Loring Air Force Base Data Center
info: LiquidCool Solutions is proposing a data center with rentable compute on the site of the former Loring Air Force Base, where 150,000 square feet will be leased from Green 4 Maine’s innovation hub. This project is unique in that it would not consume water, and instead uses polyalphaolefin oil to cool equipment.

name: Millinocket Data Center
address: Former Great Northern Paper Company site
info: Nautilus Data Technologies withdrew a proposal for a $300-million data center campus on the site of the former Great Northern Paper Company site because the developer was unable to secure a tenant due to insufficient power generation.

name: Sanford Woods Industrial and Technical Campus
info: A developer is proposing an approximately $2.16 billion technical, agricultural, and industrial campus on 1,060 acres along the Mousam River, including a data center. According to the proposal, the campus would feature an AI data center, a cold storage facility, and a large greenhouse complex. The campus would initially be powered by natural gas, with plans to transition to hydrogen fuel cells within five years. Estimated water consumption is 300,000 to 500,000 gallons per day.

name: Woodlawn Data Center
info: Security Land and Development LP, is proposing a 150-MW, 42-acre data center campus. Located near the Social Security Administration Headquarters, this project includes the construction of a new BGE electrical substation. Construction could begin as early as mid-2026.

name: Brightseat Tech Park Data Center Development
info: Lerner Enterprises is proposing a $5-billion, ~4.1-million-square-foot data center campus, dubbed the Brightseat Technology Park, on the former Landover Mall site. The project is still in the review phase

name: Laurel Data Center
companies: TPC Data Center via Mid-Atlantic LLC
info: TPC Data Center has proposed a 20-MW data center project on a ~7.56 acre site next to the Burtonsville Substation in Laurel.

name: Federick County Zoning Overlay + Quantum Frederick Park Data Center Campus
address: 5601 Manor Woods Rd, former Alcoa site
info: Data center development in Frederick County is now concentrated within a special zoning overlay of roughly 2,600 acres surrounding the former Eastalco aluminum plant north of Adamstown. The County’s zoning amendments restrict data centers to less than 1% of total land area, require buffers from residential neighborhoods and schools, and mandate farmland preservation at a five-to-one ratio for new development. Several sites within the Quantum Frederick Campus have already broken ground, including Rowan’s Bauxite. The overlay was adopted to allow large-scale data center growth in a single designated zone while preventing broader industrial expansion across rural areas of the county.

name: Piney Reach Data Center Development
address: Billingsley Road, near Charles County Landfill and recycling center
info: NAI Michael is advertising a ~432 (total) acre site for data center development on Billingsley Road.

name: Johns Hopkins Data Science and Artificial Intelligence Institute (DSAI)
address: Intersection of Wyman Park Drive and Remington Avenue.
info: Johns Hopkins University is constructing two new buildings for its Data Science and AI Institute, an advanced research center focused on artificial intelligence, high-performance computing, and data science. The facilities will be located on the Homewood Campus adjacent to the Wyman Building and the SNF Agora Institute. The project will expand the university’s capacity for interdisciplinary research and collaboration, and the buildings will include classrooms, laboratories, faculty offices, and collaborative workspaces. Construction is expected to begin in April 2026 and continue through 2029.

name: Atmosphere Dickerson Data Center
info: Atmosphere Data Centers is proposing a 360 MW, Tier III+ data center complex on 170 acres zoned for heavy industrial development at the former Dickerson coal plant. According to Atmosphere's official site, the data center will only encompass ~110 acres. The property owner, Terra Energy, is also constructing a 2,000 MWh Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) adjacent to the site.

name: Westfield Data Center Campus
info: Servistar Realties is proposing a $4-billion data center campus in Westfield that would make it the largest data center campus in the state of Massachusetts. The 10-building, 2.7 million-square-foot project on Servistar Industrial Way aims to support AI and cloud services, utilizing state tax incentives to become a major regional tech hub. The project is expected to create 400 full-time jobs and is expected to break ground in 2026.

name: Project Mitten - Stargate Project
address: 11600 W Michigan Ave, East of the Rustic Glen Golf Club
info: OpenAI, Oracle, and Related Digital are collaborating on a $7 billion data center complex east of the Rustic Glen Golf Club, as a extension of their Stargate Project. This 2.2-million-square-foot data center would be Michigan's first hyperscale data center. Oracle states that the campus is being developed responsibly, with the footprint being only 250 of 575 total acres. According to the "Michigan Courts" document linked, the Township acknowledges that the developer/tenant intends to pursue a sales and use tax exemption for the project as a qualified data center under the terms of the Michigan General Sales Tax Act, MCL 205.54ee (the “Use Tax Exemption”).

name: Project Cannoli
address: Near I-94, between Haggerty Rd and Hannan Rd
info: Panattoni Data Center Group is proposing a 800,000-square-foot data center campus on a 280-acre site, with three data processing buildings, an office and water services building, and a substation. This project is expected to have a capacity of 1 GW and will use from 2-3.6 million gallons of water a day.

name: Project Splitrock
info: Randee LLC is proposing a hyperscale data center campus on 1077 acres of land north of I-96. In September 2025, the Howell Township Planning Commission voted unanimously against rezoning the land for data centers. In December, the developer Randee LLC withdrew the rezone request. Simultaneously, the township enacted a six-month moratorium on data center development.

name: 32 Mile Road Technical Campus
address: South side of 32 Mile Road between M-53 and Powell Road
info: Prologis submitted a rezoning request for approximately 312 acres across four parcels to develop a data center campus on the south side of 32 Mile Road between M-53 and Powell Road. A decision on the request has been repeatedly postponed while the township considers implementing a zoning ordinance, with the next meeting scheduled for June 11, 2026.

name: Benton Charter Township Data Center
address: 2274 Yore Avenue, nearby Lake Michigan College
info: An undisclosed developer is proposing a $3 billion data center campus on 280 acres near Lake Michigan College. Not much information has been disclosed about this proposal, but it is suspected that this data center would source power from nearby nuclear plants. Activity for this proposal has been inactive since 2024, hence this entry is listed as delayed.

name: Microsoft Gaines Township Data Center
address: Along 76th St and Patterson Ave intersection; 7174 Patterson Ave
info: Microsoft is currently in the process of purchasing homes in the area for the campus, which according to the proposed site plan, features 7 buildings.

name: Rocklocker Kalkaska Data Center
address: Near Island Lake Rd and South River Rd
info: Rocklocker withdrew plans for a proposed 1,440 acre data center campus on public Department of Natural Resources land due to community pushback in November 2025.

name: Ypsilanti University of Michigan × Los Alamos Supercomputing Research Center
address: Intersection of Textile Road and Bridge Road, former South Hydro Park and Norfolk Southern Railway Holding properties.
info: The University of Michigan, in collaboration with Los Alamos National Laboratory, is proposing a $1.25 billion, 124-acre AI/HPC supercomputing facility at the intersection of Textile Road and Bridge Road. The campus will include a federal research facility, an academic research facility, and a new dedicated substation to exclusively power the facility. Construction is anticipated to begin in 2027 and continue through 2031. According to the University of Michigan, this project is expected to create over 200 permanent high-paying jobs in research and technical operations, 30-50 staff positions to run and maintain the computing network, and about 300 construction jobs with union labor.

name: Project Cherry Blossom
address: On the former ProMedica Hospital site off I-75 and North Dixie Highway
info: Cloverleaf Infrastructure is proposing a data center campus on 200 acres on a former ProMedica Hospital site.

name: Project Ironwood
address: Intersection of Ann Arbor Rd and Day Rd
info: Cloverleaf Infrastructure is proposing a data center on 750 acres at the corner of Ann Arbor Road and Day Road.

name: Project Flex
address: Several parcels between Milford Rd & South Hill Rd (South Hill Business Park West)
info: Verrus is proposing a 172-acre data center campus composed of six buildings totaling approximately 1.8 million square feet of floor area and a colocated substation. This project is anticipated to create 210 permanent full-time jobs once operational.

name: Deep Green Lansing Data Center
address: South Cedar Street and East Kalamazoo Street
info: Deep Green, a UK based data center company, has purchased a 3 acre parking lot in downtown Lansing, MI. The company has pledged to donate the 290,000 BTU of annual heat generated by the 24 MW data center to the city's downtown hot water system, the first of its kind in the United States. The site will use 500,000 gallons of water per year for its closed loop cooling system. The site will generate its own energy, but the type has not been specified. The project was proposed late 2025. As of April 2026, Deep Green withdrew its application for a zoning amendment that would have made the project possible. The reason for withdrawal was not made clear.

name: Pavilion Township Data Center
address: South 26th Street and East N Avenue
info: Franklin Partners is proposing a data center on farmland at the intersection of South 26th Street and East N Avenue. This proposal remains paused, largely due to community opposition, zoning issues, and concerns about energy and water consumption.

name: Sansone York Township Data Center
address: Across from Toyota Motor North American R&D Headquarters, between E Willis Rd, Platt Rd, and Bemis Rd
info: Sansone Group, a St. Louis-based real estate developer, is exploring the purchase of ~412 acres owned by Toyota for a potential hyperscale data center campus. Located between Platt Road and Willis Road, this facility would supposedly have a capacity of 1 GW. As of early 2026, no formal development application has been submitted to local authorities, and discussions about the project remain preliminary.

name: Metrobloks Southfield Data Center
address: Along Inkster Road, south of I-696
info: Metrobloks is proposing a two-story, 109,683-square-foot data center on approximately 12 acres of vacant land in Southfield. This proposal utilizes closed-loop cooling and involves the construction of a new substation for powering the facility, at the developers expense. This proposal is significantly smaller than many data center projects proposed elsewhere in Michigan.

name: DataOne Vineland
info: This massive data center project is under construction in Vineland.

name: Princeton Nurseries Data Center
address: Former Princeton Nurseries site, off Route 1
info: WRV Nurseries South Brunswick LLC has proposed a 160-acre data center project on the former Princeton Nurseries site.

name: Nutley Data Center
info: Prism Capital Partners has been granted approval to redevelop the former Hoffmann-La Roche site into a data center.

name: New Brunswick Data Center
info: Developers proposed a small 27,000-square-foot data center at a blighted site at 100 Jersey Ave. As of mid-February, the New Brunswick City Council has voted against the project, and instead plan on building a park in its place.

name: STAMP Data Center
info: Stream Data Centers, a Texas-based company, has proposed a 2.2-million-square-foot multi-billion-dollar data center at the STAMP site. The project is expected to create more than 120 permanent jobs plus hundreds of construction jobs, with groundbreaking targeted for 2026 and full operations projected by 2030.

name: Cayuga Data Campus
address: Former Cayuga Power Plant site, Lansing
info: TeraWulf is redeveloping the Miliken Station at the former Cayuga Power Plant site into a 180-acre data center campus. The campus will have an initial ~50 MW potential, scalable up to ~138 MW.

name: Lysander Data Center
info: Ranalli Super DC, LLC is proposing a large-scale hyperscale data center campus in Lysander. The project would be built on ~124 acres of land and has requested up to 300 megawatts of grid interconnection capacity, with development currently in the early planning stage.

name: Niagara Digital Campus Data Center Development
address: Somewhere near the intersection of Rainbow and John B. Daly Boulevards
info: Niagara Falls Redevelopment is proposing a ~$1.5B data center and technology campus.

name: Oneonta West End Data Center
info: Startup EcoYotta filed zoning amendment application to rezone ~153 acres from residential-ag agricultural to planned development district for a proposed data center.

name: Person County Mega Park
companies: Microsoft Corporation; Microsoft Corporation; Person County Mega Park; Duke Energy; Enbridge Gas

name: Kingsboro Data Center Campus
companies: Energy Storage Solutions (ESS); NC Renewable Energy LLC

name: Fayetteville Data Center Campus
companies: Energy Storage Solutions (ESS); PWC (Fayetteville Public Works Commission); Duke Energy

name: Lenoir Data Center Expansion
address: 708 Lynhaven Drive, and 914 Virginia St + 919 Fairview Dr parcels
companies: Google; Tapaha Dynamics LLC; Google; Blue Ridge Energy

name: Rowan County Data Center
address: Long Ferry Road (near Duke Energy Buck Steam Station)
companies: Edged (EDC Charlotte LLC); Edged (EDC Charlotte LLC); Duke Energy

name: Project Delta
companies: Engineered Land Solutions (ELS); DFC Stokes LLC; Blue Ridge Companies; CleanAIRE NC; Dan River Basin Association; National Hairston Clan; David Couch / Blue Ridge Companies; Duke Energy (Belews Creek Steam Station)

name: AWS Richmond County Campus
companies: Amazon Web Services; Amazon Web Services; Duke Energy (Sherwood H. Smith Jr. Energy Complex)

name: Statesville Data Center Campus
address: Stamey Farm Road / Hickory Highway, off I-40
companies: Compass Datacenters; Brookfield Infrastructure (owner of Compass); Compass Datacenters; Duke Energy

name: CLT20 Moores Chapel
companies: Digital Realty Trust; Digital Moores Chapel LLC; Digital Realty Trust; Digital Realty Trust

name: CLT10 Uptown Expansion
companies: Digital Realty Trust; Digital Realty Trust; Digital Realty Trust

name: Charlotte University City Data Center
companies: PowerHouse Data Centers (AREP); Town Lane; American Real Estate Partners (AREP); Town Lane; PowerHouse Data Centers; PowerHouse Data Centers; Town Lane; Duke Energy

name: 5C Charlotte Data Center
companies: 5C Group; Hypertec Cloud; 5C Data Centers; Brookfield Asset Management; Deutsche Bank; 5C Group

name: NC-1 Campus (Madison) Data Center
companies: WhiteFiber HPC; Bit Digital; Enovum Data Centers; Bit Digital; WhiteFiber HPC; WhiteFiber HPC; Nscale; Rockingham County

name: Microsoft — Catawba County Campus Cluster (Lyle Creek / Boyd Farm / Hickory / Stover North)
companies: Whiting-Turner Contracting; Morgan Construction; Ames Construction; Microsoft Corporation; Whiting-Turner Contracting; Morgan Construction; Ames Construction; Microsoft Corporation; Duke Energy

name: Greensboro Dream Center
address: 2901 E. Gate City Blvd (Gateway Research Park)
companies: ImpactData; Raeden; ImpactData; Raeden; Gateway Research Park; NC A&T State University

name: Marble Data Center Campus
companies: Core Scientific; CoreWeave; Core Scientific; CoreWeave

name: Raleigh New Build Data Center
companies: Flexential; GI Partners; Morgan Stanley Infrastructure Partners; Flexential

name: Maiden iDataCenter Campus
companies: Apple Inc.; Apple Inc.; Apple Inc.; Duke Energy; Bloom Energy
info: Apple is investing $175 million into a 237,600 square-foot data center on its existing campus on Startown Road. Apple is also constructing a 20 MW solar power facility to support its operations, making it the largest solar farm dedicated to data centers.

name: T5 Data Centers Kings Mountain
companies: T5 Data Centers; T5 Data Centers; AT&T; Walt Disney Co.; Wipro; Duke Energy
info: T5 Data Centers is expanding their existing colocation campus (?)

name: American Tower RA1
address: At ATC cellular tower site (Research Triangle region)

name: Lenoir Campus (Existing + Project Cardinal 2024)
companies: Google; Tapaha Dynamics LLC; Blue Ridge Energy

name: Natelli Investments New Hill Digital Campus
companies: Natelli Investments LLC; Protect Wake County Coalition; Natelli Investments LLC
info: Natelli Investments has withdrawn a proposal for a 250 MW data center adjacent to the Shearon Harris Nuclear Plant.

name: Tract Mooresville Technology Park
address: Between Patterson Farm Road and Rustic Road

name: Project Accelerate
companies: East John Master Development; Matthews DC LLC; Engineered Land Solutions (ELS); East John Master Development

name: Vantage New Albany Data Center Campus
info: Vantage Data Centers is currently constructing a 70-acre, $2 billion hyperscale data center campus outside New Albany, their first in the midwest. Comprised of three two-story buildings across 1.5-million square feet, the campus will draw up to 192 MW of power, and is being built in phases.

name: AWS Innovation Campus / New Albany Data Center Campus
info: AWS is developing a massive $3.5 billion, 1.25 million-square-foot, five-building data center campus on 85 acres along Beech and Miller Roads in New Albany, Ohio, with initial buildings operational as of August 2025.

name: QTS New Albany Data Center Campus
info: QTS has approval to make site-specific improvements to their existing 612,000-square-foot, data centers at their New Albany campus.

name: AWS Hillard Campus
info: The Amazon Web Service campus in Hillard is expanding its current capacity from 382 MW to rouhly 401 MW through its deal with BloomEnergy to build 228 natural gas fuel cells on the property. This project was originally set to be 7 buildings on the 141 acre plot, but has since been decreased to 5 in favor of these fuel cells. Buildings A-C have been completed and D and E remain under construction. This site is also colocated with an AEP (American Electric Power Inc.) owned and operated substation that supplies electricity to the grid. However, the new fuel cells will be owned and operated solely by AWS and there are currently no plans to connect it to the grid.

name: Project Mila / Trenton Industrial Park Data Center
address: Off Woodside and Kennel Rd, Trenton Industrial Park
info: Prologis is proposing a $7.75 million, 1-million-square-foot data center on 141 acres at the Trenton Industrial Park.

name: Hamilton Data Center
info: Logistix Property Group is proposing a 320,000-square-foot data center on ~29.2 acres along University Blvd.

name: 5C Springfield Data Center
companies: 5C Data Center (tenant context: Vultr / The Constant Company
info: 5C Data Center is developing a massive $1.3 billion, 214,000-square-foot AI hyperscale data center campus at the site of the former LexisNexis data center. This project is supported by a 15-year enterprise zone tax abatement and is expected to reach full operational capacity in 2026/2027. Note that the map boundary/area represents the entire parcel, and the data center footprint is smaller.

name: Ashville Data Center
address: North of State Route 752 toward Weigand Road
info: EdgeConneX is proposing a data center campus on a 680-acre site in Ashville. This proposal involved annexing a portion of another town into Ashville to create a single contiguous data center campus. However, in early February 2026, the Ashville Village Council rejected the annexation, restricting EdgeConneX to a 200 acre plot.

name: Piqua Data Center
address: Piqua I-75 Business and Industrial Park
info: A developer has proposed a hyperscale data center campus worth over $1 billion on a 1168-acre site off I-75.

name: Project Galaxy
address: North side of Millcreek Road, west of Vandemark Road.
info: AWS is constructing a $3 billion hyperscale data center campus on Millcreek Road. Construction is set to begin in January 2026, with completion expected by 2030.

name: Google Franlkin Furnace Data Center
info: Google is proposing a $1 billion data center campus featuring a 500,000-square foot data center in the Haverhill area. This project received approval by county commissioners for a 15-year, 75% property tax abatement tied to a payment-in-lieu-of-taxes agreement starting at $500,000 annually.

name: Van Wert Mega Site
address: Along US 30 between Marsh Rd and Gilliland Rd
info: Thor Equities has purchased a 1475 acre site for $75 million and plans to build a 500MW data center.

name: Stargate Project - Lordstown AI Data Center Campus
info: OpenAI, in partnership with SoftBank and Oracle, is transforming a former GM/Foxconn factory in Lordstown into a major hub for the $500 billion Stargate AI project.

name: Lordstown Data Center
address: Off Interstate 80 and Tod Ave SW
info: Bristolville 25 Developer LLC is proposing a $3.6 billion, 1.65 million square-foot AI data center on 133 across both Trumbull and Mahoning, adjacent to one of Project Stargate's data center complexes. The developer states that the project would include $10.8 million to update Lordstown’s water system, a community fund, the employment of 1,600 construction laborers and 120 full-time workers at the center, and tax revenue. Despite this, the Lordstown Council voted to ban data centers, and then reversed their decision to develop a moratorium on data centers for 180 days instead. Due to this, the developer filed a lawsuit against the town,

name: BitDeer Weathersfield Project
info: BitDeer has purchased a 41.8 acre site for $3.4 million that includes an interconnection agreement with FirstEnergy to supply power to the 300 MW site. The site, previously the Niles Trumbull power station, will mainly mine cryptocurrencies for the Singapore-based company.

name: Cipher Mining's "Ulysses"
info: Cipher Mining acquired a 200 MW, 195-acre site named "Ulysses" in Ohio in December 2025, marking its first expansion outside Texas to diversify into high-performance computing (HPC) and AI workloads. The site, which connects to the PJM market, is expected to be energized in late 2027. This is data center marks Cipher Mining's first expansion outside Texas.

name: Google Lockbourne
info: Google has developed a data center campus in Lockbourne, Franklin County, located near 5076 S High. The campus features multiple data center buildings constructed on the site of the former Hartman Farm, with development supported by approximately $54.3 million in local tax incentives approved in 2021.

name: Google South Columbus
info: Google has constructed a $300 million data center at the former Hartman’s Farm in Lockbourne, Ohio. The data center is expected to be operational in 2024.

name: Johnstown Cologix Data Center
address: Off Duncan Plains Rd NW, Johnstown
info: Cologix, a Denver based company, plans to build approximately across 2 million square feet data centers, eight buildings in total, on a 154 acre plot of land in Johnstown. This is the 7th data center operated by Cologix in the Colombus area.

name: Project Cosmos
info: Amazon is investing $1 billion in the construction of Project Cosmos at 33 Innovation Park in Marysville. The newest facility on the site is expected to open in 2027 and create at least 25 jobs. The current parcel is owned by the City of Marysville, and reports from a school board meeting shows renderings of the plot, confirming the location and providing acrage estimates. This site might be a part of Marysville South, another project across Industrial Parkway with AWS as its end user, that reports the same investment, timeline, and employment numbers.

name: Cologix Lewis Center Data Center
info: Cologix, a Denver based company, is in the process of constructing two 135 square foot data centers in Lewis Center, Ohio. Known as COL6 and COL5, Cologix has invested upwards of $1 billion into the project. COL5 is currently under construction and COL6 is planned.

name: Strata Expanse AI Center of Excellence
info: Strata Expanse has announced a partnership with RAVEL and Supermicro to construct a data center. This campus will be unique in that instead of directly linking to the local power grid, it will use a combination of different power sources, including natural gas, geothermal energy, cooling, hydrogen, energy storage, and solar energy to operate.

name: Hannibal Data Center Campus
info: This proposed data center is planned to be built succeeding the construction of a 485 MW natural gas power plant in a deal between DP Facilities and Long Ridge Energy, subsidiary of Fortress Transportation and Infrastructure Investors LLC. Long Ridge Energy plans to allocate about 125 acres for data center development. DP Facilities is citing it will only occupy 30 acres while Long ridge indicates the entire campus will have a capacity of 300 MW. The exact capacity has not been said on any press releases. Along with the colocated plant, the site takes advantage of existing 138kV transmission lines.

name: Bitdeer Clarington Data Center
info: Bitdeer is developing a 570 MW, high-performance computing (HPC) and AI-ready data center at the Hannibal Industrial Park. However, the developer is being sued by neighboring tenant American Heavy Plate Solutions, who claims the data center would disrupt shared infrastructure guaranteed under its long-term lease at the Hannibal Industrial Park. The landlord, Monroe County Port Authority, is also named in the suit. AHP argues Bitdeer’s 30-year lease and planned 570-MW development violate a restrictive covenant requiring tenant consent for any project that negatively affects its operations. Bitdeer had targeted completion around mid 2027, but now says timing is uncertain due to the lawsuit. Information will be updated as it is released.

name: Meta Bowling Green Data Center
address: East of Route 25 and North of Route 582
info: Meta came out with a public annoucnement that it will soon be operating a data center being built in Middle County, Ohio, north of Bowling Green. A Meta-owned entity called Liams LLC purchased more than 750 acres across 5 parcels located on both sides of Route 582, near I-75. Acoording to the Wood County Geolibrary, the most recent land purchases came in April 2025, and current satelite imagery shows that this land, around 160 acres, is what is being developed at this time. Meta has invested roughly $800 million in total to build the data center, which includes the cost of the land. The site was granted a 75% tax abatement when it was originally approved. Second hand reports of the Wood County Comissioners meeting where this took place reveals there are two buildings, each up to 400,000 square feet beign built.

name: NEO-01 / Sandusky Data Center
info: Aligned Data Centers is constructing a 300,000-square-foot data center on 129 acres at the former KBI/Delphi site. The project broke ground in 2024 and is being built in phases, with completion expected in 2026.

name: Microsoft Heath Data Center
info: Microsoft is constructing a data center off Thornwood Drive in Heath, as part of a $1 billion investment in Ohio.

name: Microsoft Hebron Data Center
info: Microsoft is constructing a data center off High Street in Hebron. Microsoft originally delayed this decision, but finally broke ground in early 2026.

name: Microsoft New Albany Data Center
info: Microsoft purchased 197 acres of land from Licking Country on March 12th, 2024 off Beech Road near Highway 62. Although research yielded no public records from the Licking County Board of Comissioners or the New Albany Planning Comission approving building plans for Microsoft Corporation on this plot, Microsoft itself has revealed that it does intend to build a data center there. The site currently has no reported capacity. Supposedly, Microsoft received a 100% tax abatement from the New Albany City Council and the company is investing $450 million in the project.

name: Urbana Data Center
address: Corner of State Route 55 and U.S. Route 68, adjacent to the Rittal facility
info: Thor Equities is considering developing a $1 billion data center adjacent to the Rittal LLC Manufacturing Facility.

name: Akron Data Center Expansion
info: ark data centers, LLC, has announced plans to invest more than $136 million to expand data center processing capabilities at its existing locations in Akron and Independence, in addition to a $4.5 million tax break awarded by the Ohio Tax Credit Authority at the recommendation of JobsOhio. This investment is anticipated to create only 10 jobs.

name: Wilmington Data Center
info: AWS has been temporarily blocked by a court order in Clinton Country Ohio, off US 68 S, south Wilmington. The land encompasses 470 acres of rezoned farm land, now set to light industrial. Amazon purchased the land in on December 10th, 2025 for $86.4 million dollars and plans to invest upwards of $4 billion in the project, spending $25 million alone for water and sewage infrustructure updates (2.5 miles of new water main and 3.5 miles of trunk sewer). A site plan presentation took place in a Planning Comission meeting on January 6th, 2026. The comission made a unanimous vote to delay the proposal during this meeting, citing the developer representative's reluctance to provide definitive information regarding water consumption, diesel generator usage, and noise impact among other items. The power consumption has also not been disclosed, but this site falls in line with AES Ohio's Fayette-Clinton 345kV transmission line project, set to roll out by 2030. The plan presentation shows 9 main buildings, a 1 million gallon backup water tower, and a new substation on the property. Plan developers include Arcadis and Bohler. The original proposal was provided by the Clinton County Port Authority during a Wilmington city council meeting on November 25th, 2025.

name: Mt. Orab Mega Site
address: South of Tri County Highway and North of Oakland Road
info: A company going under the name DB STU LLC has purchased nearly 1200 acres of land in Mt. Orab from December of 2024 to January of 2025. This came after a rezoning ordinance passed in December 2024 by the Village of Mt. Orab Council, rezoning the dubbed "Mega Cite" to heavy industrial. This site, plus another parcel bought on the other side of New Hope-White Oak Station Road brings the total land up to around 1150 acres. The village cites that electric and gas are provided by Duke Energy, water and sewer by the Village of Mt. Orab itself, and fiber through Spectrum, Horizon, and Frontier. The exact location of the site is south of Tri County Highway and north of Oakland Road in Mt. Orab. No meeting minutes were found to confirm the exact rezoning dates. Council members have signed NDAs and are not allowed to disclose plans to the residents.

name: J.M. Stuart Revitilization Project
companies: Commercial Liability; Independent Investor; Core Real Estate; High Table LLC; Energy Strike LLC; Hecate Energy
info: A data center project located on the site of a decomisssioned power plant, formally J.M Stuart, off the Ohio river in Sprigg Township originally went offline in 2018, leaving behind spent land requiring revilitization. Commercial Liability purchased the land at the end of 2019 and developed it into usable commercial land. It was purchased in parts since 2023 by Energy Strike, LLC, and was developed simultaneously with the nearby Killen Revitilization Project by High Table, LLC (both owned by the same entity). Water usage is unknown, but the developer has cited "massive" amounts with the possibility of acquiring permits to take from the Ohio River. Roughly 50 acres were sold as options to a Chicago based renewable energy company, Hecate Energy, to build power infrastructure for the site. The exact contribution from this provider is unknown. In a meeting late February, Sprigg Township passed a 12-month data center moratorium following negative feedback from the public. Capcity for this is not certain, but the Killen developer advertises up to 650 MW of power generation can be supplied within 24 months. This estimate is also applied in thsi case on the basis these are sister sites.

name: Killen Revitilization Project
companies: Commercial Liability; Independent Investor; Core Real Estate; High Table LLC; Energy Strike LLC; Hecate Energy
info: A data center project is located on the site of a decomisssioned power plant, formally Killen Generating Station, off the Ohio river in Monroe Township. The sister site is ran by Energy Strike LLC, while this data center is owned by High Table, LLC (both are owned by the same parent entity). The plant originally went offline in 2018. It was purchased soon after by Commercial Liability in 2019 and developed it into usable commercial land. It was purchased in parts since 2023 by High Table. This developer advertises up to 650 MW of power generation can be supplied within 24 months. Water usage is unknown, but the developer has cited "massive" amounts with the possibility of acquiring permits to take from the Ohio River. Roughly 40 acres were sold as options to a Chicago based renewable energy company, Hecate Energy, to build power infrastructure for the site. The exact contribution from this provider is unknown.

name: Marysville South Data Center
companies: EMH&T; New Albany Company; Anadonna LLC; Marysville Land Company LLC; 14879 Industrial Parkway LLC; Weaver Road Holdings LLC; Underhill & Hodge LLC; Mary Ann Vickner / Rosemary A. Emswiler Trust; Amazon Web Services; AES Ohio; Columbia Gas of Ohio; Union Rural Electric; City of Marysville; Marysville Exempted Village School District; Ohio Hi-Point Technical and Career Center; Community Improvement Corporation of Union County; Union County Health Department; Ohio EPA
info: The Marysville South site has roots going back to mid-2024, when the New Albany Company (through its affiliate The Marysville Land Company LLC) orchestrated the rezoning of ~591.6 acres from industrial/manufacturing zoning to a Planned Unit Development, with data centers listed as a permitted use by right. Infrastructure is already in place including an AES 138kV transmission line and access to existing water and sewer mains on Scottslawn Road and Industrial Parkway. Acting as master land developer, the New Albany Company then facilitated the CRA agreement passed by Marysville City Council on November 10th, 2025, authorizing Anadonna LLC to develop a data center on the site. The deal includes a 15-year 100% tax exemption on new structures and a PILOT (pay in lieu of taxes) payment plan back to the city, valued at ~$16 million over the 15 years. By January 2026, Anadonna LLC had purchased a 300-acre plot at 14300 Weaver Road for $205 million, the majority of the 591 acre planned site. Images from the City of Marysville's original PUD development plan suggests parcels owned by 14879 Industrial Parkway LLC and The Marysville Land Company II LLC will be absorbed into Anadonna's current ownership, consistent with the original PUD boundary. On March 11th, 2026, Amazon revealed it is the end user, and that it plans to invest upwards of $1 billion in the site by 2030 with operations beginning in 2027, but these numbers might apply to the general area, which includes Project Cosmos on the other side of the Industrial Parkway, also planed to be operated by AWS. AES will provide the site with up to 800 MW by 2033 according to a regulatory filing from February 2026, with the developer estimating 16% renewable usage and committing to remain under 90% of maximum grid capacity.

name: Buck Canyon Properties Data Center
address: East of Ginger Ridge Road and West of US Route 52
companies: Ramboll; 68 Yards LLC; Buck Canyon Properties LLC; AES Ohio; AEP; Adams County; Sprigg Township; U.S. Army Corps of Engineers; Ohio EPA
info: The Buck Canyon data center project sits on a former Dayton Power & Light landfill off U.S. Route 52 in Manchester, Sprigg Township, Adams County, Ohio. The parcel is 1,016 acres purchased in 2024 for $2.65 million by Buck Canyon Properties LLC. The development entity is 68 Yards LLC, a Chicago-based company, with the end tenant still undisclosed under NDA. AES Ohio's February 3, 2026 PJM regulatory filing confirmed power demand ramping from 100 MW in late 2028 to 1,300 MW by March 2032, which would make it one of Ohio's largest data centers at 31 times the county's entire annual energy consumption, drawing from the existing 345kV infrastructure at the Stuart Substation. Notably, while AES Ohio owns the Stuart Substation, the surrounding plant site falls within AEP's service territory, adding grid coordination complexity that PJM must resolve before full interconnection can proceed. A federal NWP 39 permit was issued February 18, 2026 authorizing stream and wetland impacts for construction. This permit was key for the developers to break ground on the project and begin building infrastructure.

name: AWS Salem Township Data Center
address: Adjacent to Susquehanna Steam Electric Station
info: Amazon Web Services is constructing a hyperscale data center campus next to Talen Energy’s Susquehanna Steam Electric Station (nuclear power plant). To power this complex, AWS has entered a $18 billion agreement with Talen Energy that will last through 2042. Capacity will be upgraded over time, initially delivering 840 MW, and then to 1,200 MW in 2029, 1,680 MW, and finally 1,920 MW in 2032.  This investment represents one of the largest private investments in Pennsylvania.

name: Project Hazelnut
address: Humboldt Industrial Park North near PA 924 & I‑81
info: NorthPoint Development of Kansas City plans Project Hazelnut, a 15-building hyperscale data center on a roughly 1,250-acre former mine site in Hazle Township, using enough power for 90,000 homes and cooled with sewer water. The project, part of Pennsylvania’s Fast Track program, is projected to create 900 permanent jobs but faces public opposition over power-line and tax-break issues. NorthPoint has already built a 1-million-square-foot warehouse nearby and now owns over 1,500 acres in the area. As of mid-November, the Hazle Township Board of Supervisors unanimously rejected the land application that would have enabled the project's construction. Developers still have have ability to appeal within 30 days, hence this proposal has been marked as rejected.

name: Wildcat Ridge AI Data Center
info: Cornell Realty Management LLC is proposing a ~$2.1 billion data center campus to be developed on 574.2 acres of mountainside land along Business Route 6 and Wildcat Road. The plans call for 14 two-story buildings, each with a 202,340-square-foot footprint. The land is currently zoned for resource conservation and medium- to high-density residential use, and the project would proceed through a data center overlay zone rather than a full rezoning. The site is located adjacent to an existing electric substation, however the proposal also includes two on-site substations measuring 326,000 square feet and 69,000 square feet, respectively. This project would use an adiabatic closed-loop system, and additional infrastructure would include a 3.75-million-gallon water storage tank and a 33,000-square-foot administration building. This proposal is the largest in the Scranton-area, and is anticipated to have a capacity of 1.6 GW, which is greater than the working capacities of any single nearby power plant.

name: Project Gravity
address: Between Business Route 6 and Eynon-Jermyn Road
info: Archbald 25 Developer LLC (affiliated with Western Hospitality Partners) has proposed a hyperscale data center campus on 195 acres in Archbald. This campus would include at least seven two-story data center buildings, each with a 138,000 sq ft footprint, and entrances on both Business Route 6 and Eynon Jermyn Road. A new 230 kV substation is also planned. The developer has proposed similar projects in Indiana and Illinois, and the site falls under Archbald’s planned data center overlay. The project has been controversial due to the displacement of residents at the adjacent Valley View Estates (Jermyn Mobile Home Village), which entered into a sales agreement with Archbald Developer II LLC on May 21, 2025, with transfer expected April 15, 2026. Roughly three dozen residents — many on fixed incomes — now face relocation, and county officials have pledged assistance. The mobile home park lies just a few hundred feet from the proposed buildings, with the main campus entrance paralleling the park’s access road. The developer, linked to multiple data center efforts in Archbald and Blakely, is expanding rapidly across Lackawanna County.

name: Blakely AI Data Center
address: Below Business Route 6 and Terrace Drive
info: Blakely Borough officials have responded to the withdrawn AI data center proposal by advancing a zoning amendment that will tightly regulate any future projects of that kind. The Borough Council voted unanimously to advertise the amendment, which confines data centers to a specific overlay district in the northwest corner of town above Business Route 6 and west of Scott Road, extending to the borough’s border with Dickson City and Scott Township. The amendment makes data centers conditional uses within that district and imposes nearly three dozen requirements before approval, including environmental, health, and sound studies—covering both audible and low-frequency noise—conducted at the developer’s expense by qualified professionals selected by the borough. Data centers will be limited to 40 feet in height, prohibited from generating on-site power, and required to occupy at least 20 acres with setbacks of 300 feet from property lines and roads and 200 feet from water bodies or wetlands. Developers must receive electrical power exclusively from Blakely Borough Electric Light Company, reimburse the borough for all related engineering and service expenses, allow quarterly inspections, and participate in emergency response training while supplying any necessary fire-suppression equipment. Other requirements include implementing water-conservation technologies such as closed-loop cooling systems, submitting all state and federal environmental permits, and following strict fencing, lighting, and vegetation standards to minimize visual impact. The amendment also defines data centers and related infrastructure, regulates energy-storage facilities, and adds zoning for short-term rentals. A public hearing on the ordinance is scheduled for November 17 at the Borough Building, with council expected to vote immediately afterward. The developers of the proposed Blakely Data Center have withdrawn their application for a zoning change after being denied the chance to present revised plans at a public meeting. While the data center itself will no longer move forward, the property will still be developed under existing zoning rules. Planned work includes tree removal, construction of a meadow-style pad for material storage, and site remediation. The developers expressed regret over missed opportunities from the canceled data center project, which they said could have generated over $119 million annually in fees for Blakely Electric, more than $2.2 million in local property taxes, created around 500 construction jobs, offered permanent high-paying positions, and funded environmental improvements such as landfill removal and mine reclamation. A local businessman had sought to rezone roughly 121 acres in Blakely from R-1 low-density residential to allow an AI data center campus with four two-story, 70-foot-tall buildings totaling about 300,000 square feet and requiring up to 1.5 GW of power—equal to the output of the Lackawanna Energy Center. The site was once planned for residential and commercial use and would have been the fifth data center proposal in the Valley View School District. The land lies within a federal opportunity zone and is eligible for $7 million in mine land reclamation funding. Blakely operates its own electric distribution system and continues to evaluate feasibility for potential future development.

name: Project Boson
info: Project Boson, formerly known as the Archbald Data & Energy Center, is separate from Project Gravity and will replace the Highway Auto Parts junkyard on Eynon Jermyn Road with a single, nearly 620,000-square-foot data center, according to recent land development filings. The project was initially planned as three two-story buildings but was later merged into one larger facility. It is led by PDC Realty LLC, managed by Jim Marzolino of Kriger Construction, who also signed the original zoning permit in April 2025 and a subsequent subdivision application in August. Marzolino, connected to both Project Boson and Project Gravity, plans to sell the Gravity site to Western Hospitality Partners under an October 2024 purchase agreement. The 83.5-acre Boson site includes a planned 200-foot tree buffer between the facility and nearby residential areas.

name: Jessup Breaker Street Data Center
info: Breaker Street Associates LLC (linked to Catalyst Commercial Development via address) has proposed a six-building data center campus totaling about 1.07 million square feet on a 131-acre site between Hill Street and the Casey Highway. Each building would rise up to 130 feet, with the campus projected to consume around 600 megawatts of power. Because the application was filed before Jessup adopted its new data center overlay zoning, the project is grandfathered under the older rules. However, a zoning appeal is underway concerning infrastructure planned in a residential zone—specifically a switch yard the borough’s zoning officer deemed non-compliant. The developer argues it should be permitted as either an accessory use or a public utility facility. In addition, the project will require a height variance since Jessup zoning limits buildings in the mixed-use district to 40 feet. On January 19th, the Jessup Zoning Hearing Board unanimously rejected the developer's appeal for zoning relief, but the developer has appealed the decision as of early February, hence this proposal is listed as delayed and not withdrawn.

name: Project Gold
address: Near I‑380 at Clifton Beach Road
info: The Project Gold data center campus, proposed by 1778 Rich Pike LLC, would develop a data center campus on roughly 1,000 acres straddling Clifton and Covington Townships. Initial plans called for nearly three dozen data centers, though revised plans show five larger buildings in Clifton ranging from 160,000 to nearly 685,000 square feet each. Gouldsboro Land LLC, linked to NE Edge Data Centers, has acquired more than 300 contiguous acres along Interstate 380 in Covington Twp., where the land falls within a newly created data center, energy and technology district. The project is currently tied up in court after a judge vacated a settlement agreement between the developer and Clifton Twp., following objections from the Clifton Twp. Zoning Hearing Board, which had unanimously denied the developer's challenge that local zoning unlawfully excluded data centers. This proposal remains very controversial.

name: East Whiteland Township Data Center
address: 13 South Bacton Hill Rd and Swedesford Rd
info: Sentinel Green Fig, LLC has proposed constructing two data center buildings on a roughly 70-acre site in East Whiteland Township at the former Foote Mineral Company property along S. Bacton Hill Road, near Swedesford Road. The site, which has been fully remediated after its prior Superfund designation, was previously approved for but never developed as a retirement community. The applicant proposes two data center buildings of ~410,000 square feet each, totaling ~828,000 square feet of building footprint. Primary access would be from Swedesford Road, with secondary emergency access to S. Bacton Hill Road, and the site would be served by public water and sewer.

name: QTS Salem Data Center
address: Adjacent to the Susquehanna Steam Electric Station
info: QTS and Blackstone Infrastructure, in partnership with PPL Corporation, announced a $25 billion plan to build several AI-oriented hyperscale data center campuses throughout Northeastern Pennsylvania. Sites will be co-located with dedicated gas-fired power plants, with operations targeted around 2030. Only one QTS site proposal has been announced so far, adjacent to the Susquehanna Steam Electric Station in Salem Township. The initial proposal included 18 buildings across three construction phases on an approximately 1,800-acre campus, but a revised master site plan was submitted in early 2026 reducing the total building count and relocating some structures. Notable changes include the removal of three buildings near the southern end of the western side of Walker Run, the removal of two buildings from the second-phase area, and the removal of two buildings from the northern third-phase section — though the three remaining northern buildings appear to be roughly double the size of those in the original plan. In March 2026, it was reported that QTS (through holding company Salem Township Holdings LLC) spent over $580 million acquiring approximately 1,700 acres from 97 local landowners. The project buildout is expected to span five to seven years.

name: AWS Keystone Trade Center Campus
address: Keystone Trade Center (former U.S. Steel Fairless Works)
info: Amazon announced plans to develop a "digital infrastructure campus" at the Keystone Trade Center in Fairless Hills as part of a statewide investment. The property is 1800 acres and was once owned by U.S. Steel.

name: CoreWeave Lancaster AI Campuses
companies: Machine Investment Group; Chirisa Technology Parks; CoreWeave
info: CoreWeave, in partnership with Blue Owl Capital, Chirisa Technology Parks, and Machine Investment Group, is advancing a multi-hundred-megawatt, multibillion-dollar (~6B committed by CoreWeave) AI data center project in Lancaster at the former RR Donnelley/LSC Communications sites at Greenfield Road and 1375 Harrisburg Pike, respectively. Construction is underway at Greenfield with expansion phases and the Harrisburg Pike campus is still in planning stages. Full buildout could support up to ~300 MW of capacity.

name: CloudHQ Berks 61 Hyperscale Campus
info: CloudHQ secured land and zoning approvals in 2024 for a ~$2B hyperscale campus with four buildings totaling ~2.4M sq ft; first 600k-sq-ft build started.

name: PAX – Pennsylvania Digital I
address: Multiple campuses planned in the Carlisle area (exact parcels TBD)
companies: PowerHouse Data Center; Pennsylvania Data Center Partners
info: PowerHouse Data Centers and Pennsylvania Data Center Partners announced a $15 billion joint venture, Pennsylvania Digital I (PAX), comprising three hyperscale campuses around Carlisle, delivering 1.35 GW of capacity with expandability up to 1.8 GW across ~700 acres. Each of the three campuses features six buildings and a dedicated 450 MW substation. As of early 2026, the project has cleared key milestones: water and sewer rights were secured in January 2026 via a $14.1 million agreement with Middlesex Township, and on March 4, 2026, the Middlesex Township Board of Supervisors unanimously approved construction of the first 450 MW substation, clearing the way for Phase 1-b construction of the first data center to begin. The project is expected to generate more than $65 million in direct tax revenue, with over $45 million allocated to the Cumberland Valley school district and local community.

name: TECfusions “Keystone Connect” AI Campus
info: TECfusions acquired ~1,395 acres from Alcoa to build a 3GW AI-ready data center campus with on-site gas generation and microgrid.

name: Springdale AI Data Center Redevelopment
info: Developers proposed a ~180 MW, 565,000-square-foot AI data center to redevelop the retired Cheswick coal plant. After months of review and mixed community response, Springdale Borough Council voted 5–2 to approve the project via a conditional use permit, as data centers are not explicitly addressed in the borough’s zoning code. In mid-December, the developer requested six zoning variances to exceed existing dimensional regulations and all were approved.

name: Homer City Energy + AI Campus
companies: Homer City Redevelopment; Kiewit; GE Vernova
info: A partnership between Homer City Redevelopment and Kiewit proposes turning the existing 2 GW facility into a 4.5 GW natural gas power plant, paired with a large AI/HPC data center campus on the 3,200-acre former coal site. GE Verona will supply seven high-efficiency hydrogen-enabled 7HA.02 gas turbines, with first deliveries expected in 2026, and the site is expected to begin producing power by 2028 (previously 2027). Most recently, environmental groups and other organizations have raised concerns about the already-permitted project. Pennsylvania's DEP issued an air quality permit in November 2025 well ahead of schedule, drawing criticism from environmental advocates who say carbon and toxic air pollution from the site could harm nearby residents. According to Homer City Redevelopment, the project will deliver up to 4.5 GW of power to AI-driven hyperscale data centers while reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 60–65% per megawatt hour compared to the former coal plant, and supply power to thousands of homes on the local grid. It will create more than 10,000 direct on-site construction jobs and approximately 1,000 permanent positions in technology, operations, and energy infrastructure, backed by an initial capital investment exceeding $10 billion, making it the largest such investment in Pennsylvania's history. Construction began as of early April 2026.

name: Panther Creek (Nesquehoning) HPC/AI Conversion
address: Panther Creek Energy Facility site (approx. 101 Panther Creek Rd)
info: Bitfarms and T5 announced intent to repurpose the Panther Creek waste-coal facility into an HPC/AI data center, leveraging existing ~1 GW infrastructure. Bitfarms has since converted its $300 million debt facility with Macquarie Group into project-specific financing for a 350 MW HPC and AI campus at the site, drawing $100 million to begin equipment purchases and site work in late 2025, with $200 million remaining for construction.

name: Fairview Crossroads Data Center
address: Approx. off I-83, I-76, & Lewisberry Rd
info: Fairview Crossroads LLC proposes a ~750,000 sq ft, ~$500M data center in Fairview Township. On June 28, the Board of Supervisors approved a zoning ordinance change to allow data centers as a conditional use.

name: Dauphin Highlands Golf Course sold for Data Center
info: The Dauphin County General Authority approved the $45.6 million sale of Dauphin Highlands Golf Course in Swatara Township to a real estate group planning to build a data center. While officials emphasized that the sale will pay off the course’s $13 million debt and deliver long-term benefits to county residents, many community members voiced concerns about noise, water use, high utility costs, and limited job creation from the proposed development. The course, which has operated at a deficit since county ownership began in 1993, will remain open until the tentative sale closing date of December 31, 2027. The sale also includes adjoining land in Steelton, and while the authority argued the purchase price was far above recorded land value, nearby residents and golfers expressed disappointment over the loss of one of the region’s best public courses. As of March 2026, Provident purchased 11 more parcels of land valuing ~$50 million.

name: Hollenback Township Data Center Development Zoning Application
address: Properties off West County Hollenback Rd
info: Iron City is possibly developing a data center at the Iron City No. 1 Power Generator and Compute Center, somewhere in the Karthaus area. The PA Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) approved Iron City Wells, LLC to construction 15 natural gas–fired engine generators to "power a compute facility", which still does not have much information public.

name: Jessup Sunnyside Data Center
address: Directly next to Lackawanna Energy Center
info: Catalyst Commercial Development, through Sunnyside Road Associates, has shifted its plans in Jessup from a warehouse to a nearly 487,000-square-foot data center on a 48.3-acre parcel along Sunnyside Road, directly beside Invenergy’s Lackawanna Energy Center. The project, which mirrors the footprint of a previously approved 334,800-square-foot warehouse but will remain under 50 feet in height, is slated to begin construction on September 1, 2026, and finish a year later. Catalyst purchased the land in 2024 for $3.4 million from Pompey Coal Company, which also owns nearby property tied to other Catalyst proposals.

name: McKees Rocks Industrial Site Data Center
address: Rock Point Industrial Site along Nichol Avenue
info: Officials in Stowe Township are considering a proposal to allow a future data center at the Rock Point Industrial site along Nichol Avenue, a strategically located riverside property. The plan is still in early stages, and current township ordinances do not permit data center operations, prompting commissioners to vote to advertise a potential ordinance change to gather public input before any decision. Township leaders view the proposal as a possible boost for jobs, tax revenue, and local economic growth, while some residents have raised questions about the project’s purpose, environmental impact, and energy consumption. Officials have stated that agencies such as the DEP and EPA would regulate environmental and safety concerns. The township board is expected to revisit and possibly vote on the proposal at its next meeting on November 10.

name: Project Hummingbird
address: Former Robena Mine site along the Monongahela River
info: A 1,400-acre hyperscale data center is proposed for Greene County at the former Robena Mine site along the Monongahela River. The project is led by Essential Utilities and International Electric Power of Pittsburgh and aims to go online in early 2029 and create 1,000+ construction jobs. On-site natural gas turbines will generate about 910 MW of power, supported by Eos Energy battery storage and grid connection for near-100% reliability. An 18-million-gallon-per-day water treatment facility will use Monongahela River water for cooling and power generation needs, with oversight by the DEP. Developers say the project will deliver energy efficiency and economic growth, positioning Pennsylvania as a national hub for AI infrastructure.

name: Zediker Station Data Center
address: Near the Interstate 79 and Interstate 79 corridors
info: CNX and JLL are collaborating to market and lease the 1,500-acre Zediker Station site in South Strabane Township, positioning it as a major data center development opportunity. The site offers 400 buildable acres with access to natural gas, water, and Remediated Mine Gas (RMG) that can support carbon-neutral power generation. JLL highlights the area’s strategic location between Ohio and Virginia data markets and its sustainable energy potential. Local officials emphasize the project’s economic promise, including new jobs and infrastructure investment.

name: Archbald I LLC Data Center Campuses
address: Between Business Route 6 and Eynon Jermyn Road
info: Developer Archbald I LLC filed two Oct. 22 applications to build a total of 22 data center buildings on two campuses that flank Project Gravity. The larger campus is located next to both Staback Park and would include 18 buildings with 150,000-square-foot footprints on a 17.8-million-square-foot (408-acre) lot and about 7 million square feet of impervious area. The smaller campus near the Archbald–Jermyn line would have four 150,000-square-foot buildings on a 2.9-million-square-foot (≈66-acre) lot with about 1.5 million square feet of paved area. Together, these two campuses sit on either side of Project Gravity’s seven-building data center campus proposal. The two new Archbald I LLC sites together would occupy about 20.7 million square feet of land, which is roughly 474 acres (408-acre south campus + ~66-acre north campus) based on the attached source. On March 27th, the Archbald Borough Council unanimously voted to deny Archbald I LLC's conditional use application for "Project Scott"/Archbald I LLC South campus, however the applicant can legally challenge the decision, hence the southern campus is listed as rejected and not withdrawn, and the northern campus is listed as delayed.

name: Former Air Products Headquarters Data Center
address: Between Hamilton Blvd and Centronia Rd
info: Air Products have submitted plans for a large data center on a 194-acre site in Upper Macungie Township, reflecting a broader shift in the Lehigh Valley from mega-warehouse projects to data centers, but the proposal remains in limbo as Air Products conducts due diligence on whether the land is suitable for that use. The site was previously approved for three warehouses that never moved forward, and while the township has agreed to delay proceedings until February, some residents have expressed frustration and officials have made clear no further extensions will be granted. As of late February, the Upper Macungie Zoning Hearing Board granted Air Products a delay until May 27 to make its appeal for a data center.

name: Pennhurst Data Center
info: Pennhurst Holdings LLC has proposed a new project to convert the former Pennhurst State School and Hospital property into a 1.3-million-square-foot data center campus. The application and preliminary site sketches detail five two-story data center buildings, a potential solar field, and an electrical substation. Township officials have confirmed that data centers are permitted under the property’s mixed-use-industrial zoning. As of mid February, the East Vincent Township Planning Commission recommended the township's board of supervisors deny the project, siting "technical deficiencies" and a failture to comply with the township's zoning ordinance. In mid March, the developer resubmitted their conditional use application, which includes an increase of data center square footage from the originally planned 1.3M to 1.9M and adds power generation.

name: Conshohocken Data Center
info: A proposal to build a large data center on a former steel mill site along the Schuylkill River was originally set for a standard zoning hearing, with the developer seeking approval for a two-million-square-foot data center project. Residents and advocacy groups had come prepared to raise concerns, but the plans were abruptly withdrawn less than an hour into the hearing after it was revealed that the developer lacked the legal standing to apply because its sale agreement for the property was not finalized. Township officials expect the developer may refile an application, which would restart the hearing process next year.

name: Storage Data Center Project
info: Next Generation Land is advertising 700 acre site for hyperscale data center development somewhere in Tioga County. According to the developer, the site combines shovel-ready land with access to over 500,000 Dth/day of firm natural gas capacity, capable of supporting more than 2.5 GW of scalable power generation, with flexibility for both behind-the-meter and grid-connected strategies. Data center activity prompted Tioga County commissioners to proactively draft an ordinance, adopted in December 2025, addressing lighting, sound and noise, energy consumption, water consumption, landscape appearance, and future decommissioning. Development has faced notable community opposition, with residents expressing concerns primarily about not wanting a data center in the county at all. No definitive development plans have been finalized.

name: Dickson City Data Center
address: Above Business Rt. 6, behind former Kmart
info: A developer linked to multiple regional data center projects has submitted sketch plans to build four large data centers on undeveloped mountainside land above Business Route 6 in Dickson City, spanning approximately 1.5 miles from the Blakely border toward Scranton. The proposal, filed by Dickson City Development LLC, involves three two-story buildings with 147,000-square-foot footprints and a fourth of unspecified size, on parcels owned by several entities connected to Jim Marzolino and the Kriger family. On February 12th, Dickson City approved a zoning ordinance restricting data center development to manufacturing-zoned districts. Dickson City has approximately 7-8 viable acres zoned manufacturing located on the opposite side of the town, effectively delaying this proposal. Until the plans are withdrawn, this proposal is marked as delayed due to the possibility of developers filing a lawsuit in the meantime.

name: West Hazleton Data Center
info: One Trinity Real Estate Investments plans to market land behind Valmont Shopping Plaza in West Hazleton for a data center, citing existing industrial zoning, prior approval for a 600,000-square-foot building, and available utilities. The borough zoning officer denied the initial application because data centers are not explicitly permitted under current zoning, prompting a planning commission public hearing scheduled for Sept. 23 and potential further review by the zoning board. Separately, One Trinity is exploring redevelopment options for the shopping plaza itself, including renovations, partial demolition, or an entirely different use.

name: Sugarloaf Township Data Center
info: Chicago-based LBT Investment Group LLC has submitted a data center proposal in Sugarloaf Township, amid another unrelated proposal for a 12-mile PPL transmission line that has drawn public criticism. Sugarloaf Township previously denied LBT’s request, prompting the company to pursue legal action and negotiations with township officials, which ultimately led to proposed amendment to the township's zoning ordinance. The township counsel indicated that zoning law requires municipalities to allow and regulate all types of land uses, even those that are unpopular.

name: Linfield Data Center
info: Limerick Town Center LLC proposed and secured approval for a land swap with the Pennsylvania Game Commission that will transfer 55 acres of State Game Lands 234 in Limerick Township and a 200-foot right-of-way to the developer in exchange for 614 acres of company-owned land across three counties, including parcels in Limerick and Bern townships and 177 acres in Edgmont Township that will become Delaware County’s first state game lands. Despite opposition from residents and a formal objection from the Limerick Township Board of Supervisors, the Game Commission approved the exchange in a 6–3 vote in Harrisburg, clearing the way for development on the Heavy Industrial–zoned site adjacent to the former Publicker distillery, where uses could include data centers. As of mid-April 2026, it was publicized that the developer finally submitted an application for a 1.7-million-square-foot data center only the original property, making the purpose of the land swap unclear.

name: Kline Township Data Center
address: "Devils Mountain", bounded by Rt-309, I-81, and the villages of Lofty and Haddock
info: Amazon Web Services has purchased approximately 350 acres on "Devil's Mountain" to construct a data center campus consisting of 10 buildings. This site was previously proposed to be developed for several warehouses, but was later resold.

name: Ransom Township Data Center
info: Scranton Materials LLC has requested a zoning amendement from Ransom Township to allow for the construction of six data centers, each with a footprints of 156,648 square feet, on Scranton Materials' property, a stone quarry site. On Jan 20th, Ransom Township's planning commission held a meeting where they reviewed and rejected the developer's appeal for zoning relief, but as of early March, the developer has filed two separate legal challenges against the township over this ruling.

name: Atlas Industrial / South Whitehall Township Hyperscale Data Center Campus
address: 2493 N. Cedar Crest Blvd; across the street from Parkland High School
